questions.specification: Which group of functional areas is typically connected through the shared database of a Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) system?

options.content: Legal counsel, financial auditing, and human resource management
Product design, material requirements planning, and shop-floor production
Demographic research, retail marketing, and product disposal
Hand-sketching, manual assembly, and face-to-face sales

questions.specification:
A manufacturer of custom-fit mouthguards for school sports currently relies on manual steps: staff take dental impressions, hand-trim the moulds, heat-form plastic sheets, and engrave names. Orders surge at the start of each term and errors in sizing lead to remakes.

The company is considering a computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) cell that links: intra-oral 3D scanning, CAD design of the mouthguard, CAM toolpath generation, a CNC trimming/finishing machine, a small robotic arm for part handling, and an automated inspection station that checks dimensions against the CAD model.

![A technician viewing a 3D scan on a monitor beside a small CNC machine and a compact robotic arm placing a clear plastic mouthguard onto a conveyor for inspection.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/e0e147a0-5350-4bef-9406-4f01a48cda6e.png)

Figure 1: Proposed CIM cell concept

![A close-up of a computer screen showing a CAD model of a mouthguard with highlighted measurement points and an inspection pass/fail panel.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/0eaa9968-1d55-4646-91a3-59f46b95ce76.png)

Figure 2: CAD model used for automated inspection

parts.content: List two components or technologies that would typically be integrated in a CIM system for producing the mouthguards.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a valid CIM component/technology (e.g., CAD, CAM, CNC, robotics, automated inspection/CAI, sensors, PLC/SCADA, integrated database/networking). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second valid CIM component/technology. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how CIM could be applied to handle high order volumes at the start of term while still producing mouthguards customised to each student.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains use of a digital workflow linking scan data to CAD/CAM so each product is customised without retooling. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how automation/integration (e.g., robotic handling, automated scheduling, rapid changeover, automated inspection) increases throughput to meet peak demand. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline one advantage and one disadvantage of moving from the current manual process to a CIM cell for this product.
parts.markscheme:
- States an appropriate advantage linked to CIM (e.g., improved consistency/quality, reduced labour per unit, faster lead times, better traceability, fewer remakes). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- States an appropriate disadvantage linked to CIM (e.g., high capital cost, need for skilled technicians/training, dependence on software/data accuracy, downtime/cybersecurity risk, less flexibility for unusual cases). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how integrating CAD, CAM and automated inspection in a CIM system could reduce remakes and improve the efficiency of the manufacturing process for the mouthguards.
parts.markscheme:
- Describes CAD as the source of an accurate digital model/specification for the customised mouthguard. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes CAM using the CAD data to generate machine instructions/toolpaths, reducing manual interpretation errors. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains automated inspection/CAI comparing measured part data to the CAD model/tolerances to detect defects early. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links integration/feedback (e.g., traceability, automatic parameter adjustments, fewer reworks/remakes, reduced waste and cycle time) to improved efficiency. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Forge manufactures aluminium bicycle crank arms. The company is planning to modernise its production by introducing a computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) system that links design, planning, manufacture and inspection.

The proposed workflow is:
1. A designer creates the crank arm in a CAD system and exports the model.
2. CAM software generates CNC toolpaths for a milling machine.
3. A central database stores the bill of materials (BOM), routings and machine programs.
4. Barcodes on pallets identify each part as it moves via a conveyor to machining, washing and inspection.
5. A coordinate measuring machine (CMM) checks key dimensions and automatically updates the quality records.
6. The system can schedule jobs, reorder material when stock is low and produce traceability reports.

![A factory cell showing a CNC milling machine behind a safety guard, a conveyor with barcode-tagged pallets, a robot arm loading parts into the CNC, a CMM inspection station with a probe, and a nearby computer terminal representing a central CIM database and production schedule.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/60082fa2-4c40-4e6a-bd7b-cb3165928021.png)

![A close-up view of a barcode label on a pallet next to an aluminium crank arm blank, with arrows indicating data flow from the scanner to a central system and then to the CNC machine and CMM station.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/07068fd9-1d3b-4b27-a8b6-57ebe2ffaa60.png)

parts.content: List two elements or technologies that could be part of Forge’s CIM system.
parts.markscheme:
- CAD used to create/modify product designs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CAM used to generate toolpaths/CNC programs from CAD models <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CNC machine tools controlled by computer programs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Robotics/robot arms for loading/unloading/handling parts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Automated material handling (e.g., conveyors/AGVs) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Barcoding/RFID for identification and tracking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CMM/automated inspection linked to quality records <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Central database/MRP/ERP integrating BOM, routings, stock and schedules <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline one application of CIM in this factory beyond just machining the part.
parts.markscheme:
- Automated production planning/scheduling (e.g., prioritising jobs, allocating machines) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Inventory control and automatic reordering based on stock levels/usage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Real-time tracking/traceability of parts through barcode/RFID logging <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Automated inspection/quality management where CMM results are captured and stored automatically <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Automatic generation/management of documentation (e.g., work instructions, batch records) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how introducing CIM could affect Forge’s operation, including advantages and disadvantages.
parts.markscheme:
- Integration of CAD–CAM–CNC reduces re-entry of data and speeds up changeovers/new product introduction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher consistency/accuracy from automated control and repeatable CNC programs, reducing variation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved quality assurance through automated inspection (e.g., CMM feedback) and better records/traceability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Better productivity/throughput from automation of handling/scheduling and reduced idle time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced labour for repetitive tasks; roles shift toward programming, maintenance and supervision <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High initial capital cost (machines, software, networking, integration) and ongoing licensing/support <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Need for skilled staff/training; risk of skills gap and dependence on specialist technicians <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Downtime risk: a system/network/software failure can stop multiple stages of production at once <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced flexibility for very small batches/custom one-offs if setup/programming overhead is significant <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cybersecurity/data integrity risks (e.g., tampered programs/incorrect database data causing scrap) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 6 marks</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following is **not** a characteristic of Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)?

options.content: Integration of design and manufacturing processes

questions.specification:
A mid-sized bicycle manufacturer, VeloForge, produces commuter and e-bike frames. The firm is considering a shift to **computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM)** to link its design studio, production equipment, warehouse and order processing.

VeloForge’s proposal includes:
- customers configuring a frame online (size, colour, accessory mounts)
- automatic generation of a digital product file for the factory
- robots and CNC machines cutting and welding tubes
- sensors collecting data during welding and painting
- automatic stock updates and scheduling of work orders

Figure 1 shows a prototype screen from VeloForge’s online configurator.

![A web configurator for a bicycle frame showing size selection, colour picker, accessory mount toggles, and a live 3D preview of the frame. A panel lists “Estimated build time” and an automatically updated price as options are selected.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/99c3be38-f1dd-475c-bbeb-5b6ad7184296.png)

Figure 2 shows an example of the order summary sent to the factory.

![An order summary page listing the chosen frame size, paint colour, accessory mounts, and the final itemised price. A section titled “Manufacturing file generated” shows a file name and timestamp, and a status line reading “Sent to production queue”.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/a66d5d9d-4d9b-450c-8eba-1cd2e5823076.png)

parts.content: State two components or technologies that are typically integrated within a CIM system.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a valid CIM-integrated component/technology (e.g., CAD, CAM, CNC/robotics, automated materials handling, sensors/IoT, automated storage/retrieval, ERP/MRP integration, barcodes/RFID, FMS) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second valid CIM-integrated component/technology <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how CIM could be applied to VeloForge’s customer configurator to link the customer’s choices to factory production.
parts.markscheme:
- Customer selections are converted into a digital design/specification (e.g., CAD model/BOM/routing) automatically <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The specification is transmitted to production systems (e.g., CAM instructions to CNC/robots, automatic job scheduling/materials call-off) to manufacture the customised frame <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline one advantage and one disadvantage for VeloForge of adopting CIM for frame production.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage linked to CIM (e.g., faster throughput, higher consistency/precision, lower unit costs at volume, fewer errors via data integration, greater flexibility/mass customisation, improved traceability) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage linked to CIM (e.g., high capital cost, need for skilled staff/training, cyber/security risk, breakdowns causing system-wide stoppage, inflexibility if software/data errors, implementation disruption) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how CIM could help VeloForge coordinate design, production and inventory so that customised frames are produced accurately and on time.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains end-to-end integration (e.g., CAD updates flow to CAM/production instructions without re-entry, reducing transcription/specification errors) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains automated scheduling/production control (e.g., real-time machine status and queue management to prioritise orders and meet due dates) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains inventory/material coordination (e.g., BOM triggers automatic stock checks, barcode/RFID updates, reordering, and materials movement to the correct workstations) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains monitoring/feedback (e.g., sensor data/traceability flags deviations, enabling quick correction and preventing defective items reaching dispatch) <marks>1 mark</marks>
